attributes.stem:
A 45-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a six-month history of urinary leakage when coughing, sneezing, or exercising. She finds this particularly bothersome during her aerobics classes. She has had two vaginal deliveries, the most recent eight years ago. She has a regular period each month and has no issues with this currently. Her cervical screening test is up to date. She has no other significant medical history and takes no regular medications. She has never sought medical attention for this problem before.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Abdominal examination is unremarkable. Pelvic examination reveals normal external genitalia with no evidence of prolapse. Stress test demonstrates urine leakage with coughing. Urinalysis is negative for infection.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step for this patient?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with classic symptoms of stress urinary incontinence (SUI), which is the involuntary leakage of urine with increased intra-abdominal pressure such as during coughing, sneezing, or exercise. The diagnosis is supported by the demonstration of stress incontinence on examination.

Supervised pelvic floor exercises are the first-line treatment for SUI. When properly taught and performed regularly, these exercises can significantly improve symptoms in up to 70% of women. A typical program involves at least three months of regular exercises under the guidance of a physiotherapist specialising in women's health.

Mid-urethral sling surgery is an effective treatment for SUI but should only be considered after conservative measures have failed. It would be inappropriate to proceed directly to surgery without first trying non-invasive options.

Oxybutynin and other anticholinergic medications are used for urgency incontinence but are not effective for pure stress incontinence. These medications work by reducing bladder overactivity, which is not the underlying problem in SUI.

Vaginal oestrogen cream may be helpful in postmenopausal women with urogenital atrophy, but this patient is premenopausal and has no indication for local oestrogen therapy.

Urodynamic studies are not routinely required before initiating conservative management for uncomplicated SUI. They may be considered if initial management fails or if there is diagnostic uncertainty.

Other conservative measures that should be discussed include weight loss if appropriate, avoiding caffeine, and timing of fluid intake. The patient should also be counselled about the natural history of SUI and the importance of adherence to pelvic floor exercises for optimal results.
learningPoints: Conservative measures including pelvic floor exercises are first-line treatment for stress urinary incontinence.
Mid-urethral sling surgery should only be considered after a trial of conservative management.
Anticholinergic medications are not effective for pure stress urinary incontinence.
references: Royal Australian and New Zealand College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RANZCOG). Stress urinary incontinence [Internet]. Melbourne: RANZCOG; [cited 2024 Nov 25]. Available from: https://ranzcog.edu.au/wp-content/uploads/Stress-Urinary-Incontinence.pdf
